title: Podman Containers not restarting when crashing or when Healthcheck fails
body:
### Issue Description

I have an Issue where Containers are NOT getting restarted, even though `Restart=always` was correctly specified in the relevant `[Service]` Section.

I already had to implement a minimal [Supervisor Container](https://github.com/luckylinux/podman-container-supervisor) to push the Pod "off a Cliff" in case the Health Check fails (and the required Tools, e.g. `curl`, are not available in the Application Container).

Now I basically need to implement a Systemd Supervisor, since the Supervisor Container just crashes and the entire Pod gets killed, but nothing is restarted 😕.

I would expect that the Container gets restarted automatically (as it's supposed to) without much extra Attention, whether that is triggered by:
- Health Check (using `HealthCmd`)
- Container Crashes on its own

Using my [Supervisor Container](https://github.com/luckylinux/podman-container-supervisor) both Approaches are possible, and both the Health Check Approach and the internal Loop fail to trigger a restart of the Container 👎.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

`example-supervisor.container`:
```
[Unit]
Description=Example Supervisor Container
#OnFailure=podman-pod-autorestart@example.service

# These are some Settings that were attempted but were in the End disabled
#StartLimitIntervalSec=15
#StartLimitBurst=15

[Service]
#ExecStopPost=/bin/bash -c "systemctl --user restart podman-pod-autorestart@example.service"

# These are some Settings that were attempted but were in the End disabled
Restart=always
#RestartSec=5s
#ExecStartPre=sleep 1

[Container]
ContainerName=example-supervisor

Pod=example.pod
StartWithPod=true

# Generic Environment File
EnvironmentFile=.env

# Other Environment Settings
Environment=HEALTH_DEBUG=1
Environment=HEALTH_START_PERIOD=10
Environment=HEALTH_INTERVAL=10
Environment=HEALTH_RETRIES=2
Environment=HEALTH_INTERNAL_LOOP=1
Environment=HEALTH_OUTPUT_STREAM=stderr
Environment=APPLICATION_HOSTNAME=example.MYDOMAIN.TLD

Image=podman-container-supervisor:alpine-base-latest
Pull=missing

# Health Check Approach - might require some Updates
#HealthCmd=["/healthcheck.sh"]
#HealthStartPeriod=15s
#HealthRetries=5
#HealthInterval=15s
#HealthOnFailure=kill
#HealthLogDestination=/home/podman/containers/quadlets/example/healthcheck
#Environment=HEALTH_DEBUG=0
#Environment=HEALTH_START_PERIOD=0
#Environment=HEALTH_INTERVAL=0
#Environment=HEALTH_INTERNAL_LOOP=0
#Environment=HEALTH_OUTPUT_STREAM=stdout,stderr

# Stop Container Immediately
# StopSignal=SIGKILL

# Healthcheck Script
Volume=./healthcheck.sh:/healthcheck.sh:ro,z
```

`healthcheck.sh` (passes):
```
#!/bin/bash

# Abort on Error
set -e

# Load Library
source "/opt/supervisor/functions.sh"

# Echo
log_info "Running healthcheck.sh"

# Ping hub.docker.io (IPv4)
is_http_reachable_ipv4 "https://hub.docker.com"

# Check that HTTP Status Code is 200
http_status_code=$(curl_http_status_code "https://${APPLICATION_HOSTNAME}")
curl_return_code=$?

if [[ ${http_status_code} -ne 200 ]]
then
    # Error
    log_error "CURL Health Check failed with CURL Return Code ${curl_return_code} and HTTP Status Code ${http_status_code}"

    # Exit abnormally
    exit 1
fi

# Exit Normally
exit 0
```

`healthcheck.sh` (fails):
```
#!/bin/bash

# Abort on Error

# Check that HTTP Status Code is 200

if [[ ${http_status_code} -ne 200 ]]
then
    # Error
    log_error "CURL Health Check failed with CURL Return Code ${curl_return_code} and HTTP Status Code ${http_status_code}"

    # Exit abnormally
    exit 1
fi

# Exit Normally
exit 0
```

`example.pod`:
```
[Pod]
PodName=example

# HTTP
PublishPort=80:80/tcp
PublishPort=443:443/tcp
PublishPort=443:443/udp

# Network Mode
Network=pasta

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Clone and build [Supervisor Container](https://github.com/luckylinux/podman-container-supervisor). Alternatively you can implement something more minimal off a minimal Alpine or Debian Image I think.
2. Start the Container and let it crash / fail the Health Check

### Describe the results you expected

I would expect the Container to just restart as intended.

I had to play with a custom Service and extra Script plus a bit of fiddling around with `OnFailure` and `ExecStopPost` plus playing a bit with `StartLimitIntervalSec=10` and
`StartLimitBurst=1` in order to avoid creating a Restart Loop (which would otherwise be triggered since `example-supervisor.service` will trigger the Restart of the `example-pod.service` Pod via `podman-pod-autorestart@example.service`, but then that will in turn trigger the restart of `example-supervisor.service`.


`${USER}/.config/systemd/user/podman-pod-autorestart@.service`:
```
[Unit]
Description=Automatically Restart Podman Pod <%i>
StartLimitIntervalSec=10
StartLimitBurst=1

[Service]
RemainAfterExit=yes
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c "${USER}/podman-tools/podman_pod_autorestart.sh %i start"
ExecStop=/bin/bash -c "${USER}/podman-tools/podman_pod_autorestart.sh %i stop"

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

`${USER}/podman-tools/podman_pod_autorestart.sh`:
```
#!/bin/bash

# Determine toolpath if not set already
relativepath="./" # Define relative path to go from this script to the root level of the tool
if [[ ! -v toolpath ]]; then scriptpath=$(cd "$( dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" )" && pwd ); toolpath=$(realpath --canonicalize-missing ${scriptpath}/${relativepath}); fi

# Load functions
source ${toolpath}/functions.sh

# Define user
if [[ ! -v user ]]
then
   user=$(whoami)
fi

# Define Pod Name as the first Input Argument
podname="$1"

# Define Action as the second Input Argument
action="$2"

if [[ "${action}" == "start" ]]
then
    # Echo
    echo -e "Trigger restart of Podman Pod <${podname}>"

    # Echo
    echo -e "Reloading Systemd"

    # Reload Systemd
    systemctl --user daemon-reload

    # Reset all relevant Failed Services
    systemctl --user reset-failed ${podname}-*.service

    # Wait a bit
    sleep 5

    # Restart Pod
    systemctl --user restart ${podname}-pod.service
elif [[ "${action}" == "stop" ]]
then
    # Echo
    echo -e "Stopping auxiliary Systemd Unit podman-pod-autorestart@${podname}.service (no Action performed)"
fi
```

comments.body: I can fix the container restart issue when crashing or healthcheck fails. If **maintainer** thinks this needs fixing, please assign to me.

This appears to be a configuration issue rather than a Podman bug. The `Restart=always` directive in the `[Service]` section is being blocked by systemd's default restart limits. You can see in your logs that systemd schedules the restart but then immediately stops the service without actually restarting it. Could you provide the contents of `example-supervisor.service` and the output of `podman inspect` for that container?

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).

@Honny1: everything is of course Possible, however I also wouldn't call it intuitive.

I thought about some `StartLimitIntervalSec` or `StartLimitBurst` (and I tried "playing" with them) but also that didn't seem to solve the Issue 😕. Of course it might be another Parameter playing a Role.

Either the Defaults of Podman/Systemd aren't sane or the Systemd Configuration that I used to fix a previous Problem with Systemd restarting Containers too quickly (sub-Second Restarts) is incorrect (see at the End of this Comment).

I find it however irritating that Systemd is not very explicit about what it does and why. Systemd should be more talkative and just write something in the Logs about some Start/Restart that happened too quickly, if that was the Case. Here I just see that the Container will be restarted and 1 Second after that, it gets stopped, without any clear Explanation (or at all).

I'll provide an even more minimal Example to reproduce, as requested by the Bot.

This Example is just an `alpine` Image Container, a basically empty Pod plus a `healthcheck.sh`Script (POSIX Shell, since BASH isn't installed in the default `alpine`Image).

`example.pod`:
```
[Pod]
# Pod Name
PodName=example

# Network Mode
Network=pasta

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

`example-supervisor.container`:
```
[Unit]
Description=Example Supervisor Container

[Service]
Restart=always

[Container]
ContainerName=example-supervisor

Pod=example.pod
StartWithPod=true

Image=docker.io/library/alpine:latest
Pull=missing

# Other Environment Settings
Environment=HEALTH_START_PERIOD=15
Environment=HEALTH_INTERVAL=10
Environment=HEALTH_RETRIES=2

# Simulate Action
# Possible Values:
# - pass
# - fail
Environment=HEALTH_SIMULATE_ACTION=fail

# Healthcheck Script
Volume=./healthcheck.sh:/healthcheck.sh:ro,z

# Entrypoint
Entrypoint=/healthcheck.sh

# Entrypoint for Debugging
# Entrypoint=/bin/sh
# PodmanArgs=-it

# Stop Signal (no need to wait 10s for stopping this minimal Container)
StopSignal=SIGKILL
```

`healthcheck.sh`:
```
#!/bin/sh

# Echo
echo "Start Health Check Simulation"

# Abort on Error
set -e

# Echo
echo "Waiting ${HEALTH_START_PERIOD}s to simulate Blanking Time to start the actual Application"

# Wait a bit
sleep ${HEALTH_START_PERIOD}

# Initialize Counter
health_counter=0

while true
do
    # Simulate Action
    if [ "${HEALTH_SIMULATE_ACTION}" = "pass" ]
    then
        # Do nothing
        x=1

        # Echo
        echo "Health Check OK"
    else
        # Increment Counter
        health_counter=$((health_counter+1))

        # Echo
        echo "Health Check Failed. The Health Check Counter is now at [${health_counter}/${HEALTH_RETRIES}]"

        if [ ${health_counter} -ge ${HEALTH_RETRIES} ]
        then
            # Echo
            echo "Terminate Healthcheck Script due to Health Counter exceeding Threshold ${HEALTH_RETRIES}"

            # Exit with Error
            exit 1
        fi
    fi

    # Wait
    sleep ${HEALTH_INTERVAL}
done

# Exit Normally
exit 0
```

The issue is that the pod service will not restart; once it is removed, the container is no longer needed. I tested this without the pod, and it restarted as expected.

I recommend using a healthcheck to achieve your desired behavior: https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-healthcheck.1.html

I am moving this to a discussion, as this appears to be a configuration issue rather than a bug. Thanks!
